average_salary: 6-45 LPA in India depending on company tier and experience; 90k-250k USD+ in the US at product companies
certifications: Coursera Data Structures and Algorithms Specialization - UC San Diego (Paid, financial aid available)
future_scope: As data volumes grow and real-time systems become the norm, efficient algorithms remain non-negotiable. DSA skills increasingly overlap with distributed systems design, where the same complexity-analysis mindset applies to sharding, load balancing, and caching strategies.

frequently_asked_questions: Explain time and space complexity of your solution
Walk me through your thought process
hr_questions: Tell me about a time you solved a difficult technical problem
Why do you want to join this company?

overview: Data Structures and Algorithms (DSA) is the study of organizing data efficiently and designing step-by-step procedures to solve computational problems. It forms the theoretical and practical backbone of computer science, determining how fast and how efficiently software can run at scale.
prerequisites: Any one programming language (Python, Java, C++)

projects.title: Custom Data Structure Library
projects.description: Implement your own Stack, Queue, LinkedList, and BST from scratch without built-in libraries.

projects.title: Pathfinding Visualizer
projects.description: A web app visualizing BFS, DFS, and Dijkstra's algorithm on a grid.

projects.title: In-Memory Database Engine
projects.description: Build a simplified key-value store with indexing using B-Trees/hashing and an LRU eviction policy.

resume_tips: List DSA-heavy projects with GitHub links, not just course completions
Quantify impact: 'optimized query from O(n^2) to O(n log n), reducing latency by 40%'
Mention specific data structures/algorithms used per project, not just 'built an app'

skills.name: Complexity Analysis
skills.description: Ability to determine Big-O time and space complexity of any algorithm.
skills.importance: Critical -- interviewers grade on this explicitly.

skills.name: Recursion & Backtracking
skills.description: Solving problems by breaking them into smaller identical subproblems.

skills.name: Graph Algorithms
skills.description: BFS, DFS, shortest path, and connectivity algorithms.

skills.name: Dynamic Programming
skills.description: Optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems technique.

topics.name: Arrays
topics.description: A contiguous block of memory storing elements of the same type, accessible via an index in constant time.
topics.importance: Foundation for nearly every other data structure; the single most common interview topic.

resources.notes: Focus on understanding why access is O(1) but insertion/deletion is O(n).
resources.cheat_sheet: Access O(1), Search O(n), Insert/Delete O(n) worst case

practice_questions: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock
projects: Build a simple spreadsheet-like grid data viewer
interview_questions: When would you choose an array over a linked list?

topics.name: Linked Lists
topics.description: A linear data structure where elements (nodes) are linked using pointers rather than stored contiguously.
topics.importance: Tests pointer manipulation skills; frequently used in system design (LRU cache) and OS-level structures.

resources.notes: Practice drawing pointer diagrams on paper before coding.
resources.cheat_sheet: Access O(n), Insert/Delete at head O(1), Search O(n)

projects: Implement a music playlist with next/previous navigation
interview_questions: Singly vs doubly linked list tradeoffs
How does Floyd's cycle detection work?
Reverse a linked list iteratively and recursively

topics.name: Stacks & Queues
topics.description: Stacks are LIFO (Last-In-First-Out); Queues are FIFO (First-In-First-Out) linear structures used for ordered processing.
topics.importance: Core to parsing, backtracking, BFS/DFS traversal, and expression evaluation.

resources.notes: Monotonic stacks are a recurring interview pattern -- practice them explicitly.

projects: Build a basic calculator supporting operator precedence
interview_questions: Implement a queue using two stacks
What is a monotonic stack and when do you use one?

topics.name: Trees & Binary Search Trees
topics.description: Hierarchical structures where each node has child nodes; BSTs maintain sorted-order properties for efficient search.
topics.importance: Foundation for databases (B-Trees), file systems, and a very common interview category.

resources.notes: Master the three DFS traversal orders (pre/in/post) before moving to BFS.
resources.cheat_sheet: Balanced BST: Search/Insert/Delete O(log n); Unbalanced worst case O(n)

projects: Build a file-system explorer using a tree structure
interview_questions: Difference between BST and balanced BST (AVL/Red-Black)
How does in-order traversal of a BST relate to sorting?

topics.name: Hashing
topics.description: Maps keys to array indices via a hash function, enabling average O(1) lookups, insertions, and deletions.
topics.importance: Used everywhere from caching to database indexing; extremely common in interview optimization questions.

resources.notes: Understand collision resolution: chaining vs open addressing.
resources.cheat_sheet: Average O(1) access/insert/delete; worst case O(n) with poor hash function

projects: Build a simple in-memory key-value cache with expiry
interview_questions: How do hash collisions get resolved?
Why is a good hash function important for performance?

topics.name: Graphs
topics.description: A set of nodes (vertices) connected by edges, used to model networks, relationships, and paths.
topics.importance: Models real-world systems: social networks, maps, dependency resolution; core to advanced interviews.

resources.notes: Learn BFS for shortest path in unweighted graphs, Dijkstra for weighted, and Union-Find for connectivity.
resources.cheat_sheet: BFS/DFS O(V+E); Dijkstra O((V+E) log V); MST (Kruskal/Prim) O(E log V)

projects: Build a shortest-route finder between cities on a small map dataset
interview_questions: BFS vs DFS -- when to use which?
Explain Dijkstra's algorithm and its limitation with negative weights

topics.name: Dynamic Programming
topics.description: An optimization technique that solves complex problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems and storing results to avoid recomputation.
topics.importance: Widely regarded as the hardest and most differentiating interview topic at top companies.

resources.notes: Always start by writing the brute-force recursive solution, then add memoization, then convert to tabulation.
resources.cheat_sheet: Time complexity typically O(states x transitions); trade space for time via memoization

projects: Build a text-diff tool using edit-distance DP
interview_questions: How do you identify a DP problem during an interview?

why_important: DSA is the single most heavily tested skill in technical interviews at product-based companies (Google, Amazon, Microsoft, Adobe) and increasingly at service companies too. Beyond interviews, strong DSA fundamentals directly translate to writing code that scales -- the difference between a feature that works for 100 users and one that works for 100 million.
